topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lilies, impossibly vibrant against the muted grey of my apartment wall, sat on the coffee table. No note. Just the heavy, velvety heads demanding attention. I picked one up, the stem cool against my fingertips, and traced the delicate curve of a petal. My jaw tightened, a familiar clenching starting deep in my chest. I hadn't expected anything. The silence in the room felt amplified, pressing in on my ears. Why send something so overtly expressive without an explanation? A knot formed just beneath my ribs, tightening with every breath I took. I set the flower down too quickly, the small clink of ceramic sounding jarringly loud. I stared at the empty space where the card should have been, my gaze darting around the room as if expecting a hidden message in the pattern of the rug.

[story 2]

It was a Tuesday, and the delivery person, a young man with an overly bright smile, had just placed a bouquet of deep purple irises on my doorstep. No accompanying paper. I stood by the door, my hands gripping the frame until my knuckles were white. A strange flutter started in my stomach, a nervous energy that made my palms slick. I glanced down the street, half-expecting someone to be watching, judging this sudden, unsolicited gesture. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to regulate the quickening rhythm of my pulse. The flowers seemed too bold for the quiet anxiety swirling inside me. I didn't move to take them in; instead, I backed away slowly, the scent of the blossoms suddenly overwhelming, a sharp, unfamiliar intrusion into my carefully constructed solitude.

[story 3]

The arrangement was lush, a chaotic mix of roses and deep crimson foliage. I found them tucked into the hallway, left on the welcome mat. I didn't rush to examine them. I stood there, rooted to the spot, my feet feeling strangely heavy on the wooden floor. My gaze kept flickering towards the door, as if expecting the person who sent them to reappear, demanding an answer. A dull ache settled behind my eyes, making the edges of the room swim slightly. I reached out, my fingers brushing against a thorny stem, and pulled back almost immediately, as if the contact itself were too much to bear. The lack of context was a heavy weight, pressing down on my shoulders, making it difficult to draw a full, steadying breath.
